rawIndexableTextContent: A CloudKit-backed cloud file system.  Overview Stores files as CKRecord objects with CKAsset attachments. Supports custom zones, server change token caching for efficient incremental fetches, and CKShare-based sharing. Preserves exact CKRecord type names (CDEFileSystemNode, CDEItem) and field names from the original Objective-C implementation for backwards compatibility. Setup Enable the iCloud capability in your Xcode project Check CloudKit under iCloud services Create or select a CloudKit container (e.g. iCloud.com.yourcompany.yourapp) Initialize the file system:  No API keys or OAuth tokens are needed — CloudKit uses the device’s iCloud account automatically. Schema Versions CloudKitSchemaVersion.v1 — Original schema with separate CDEDataFile records. Use this for backward compatibility with existing Ensembles 2 CloudKit data. CloudKitSchemaVersion.v2 — Simplified schema without CDEDataFile records. Recommended for new apps. Database Scope Use usePublicDatabase: false (private database) for most apps. The private database syncs across the user’s own devices via their iCloud account. The public database is shared across all users and requires no iCloud sign-in, but has different quota limits. Publishing the Schema CloudKit schemas must be deployed to production before your app ships. After testing in the Development environment: Open CloudKit Console Select your container Go to Deploy Schema to Production Review the record types and deploy The schema is created automatically in Development when you first run the app, but it won’t be visible to production users until you deploy it. Sharing For CKShare-based sharing with a custom zone:
title: CloudKitFileSystem
summary: A CloudKit-backed cloud file system.

rawIndexableTextContent: A CloudKit-based cloud backend for Ensembles. Overview EnsemblesCloudKit provides CloudKitFileSystem, an implementation of the Ensembles CloudFileSystem protocol that stores sync data in iCloud using CloudKit. It handles file upload, download, and directory management through CloudKit record operations. CloudKit is the recommended backend for most apps — it requires no server setup, no authentication UI, and syncs automatically across all of a user’s iCloud devices. Xcode Setup Open your Xcode project and select the app target. Go to Signing & Capabilities and click + Capability. Add iCloud. Check CloudKit under iCloud services. Create or select a CloudKit container (e.g., iCloud.com.yourcompany.yourapp). The container identifier must be unique across all apps. Quick Start  Or use the container for a simpler setup:  Database Scope CloudKit offers three database scopes: Private database (default) — Syncs across the current user’s own iCloud devices. Data is only accessible to the signed-in iCloud account. This is the recommended scope for most apps. Public database — Data is accessible to all users of the app, even without iCloud sign-in. Uses different quota limits (per-app rather than per-user). Useful for shared reference data. Shared database — Used with CKShare for sharing specific data between users.  Schema Versions CloudKit uses record types to store sync data. Two schema versions are available: .v1 — The original schema with three record types: CDEFileSystemNode, CDEItem, and CDEDataFile. Compatible with Ensembles 2 CloudKit data. .v2 — A simplified schema that removes the CDEDataFile record type. Slightly more efficient but not readable by E2 peers using CloudKit. The default is .v1 for backward compatibility. Use .v2 for new apps that don’t need E2 compatibility. Publishing Schema to Production You must deploy the CloudKit schema to production before submitting your app to the App Store. Without this step, the app will fail to sync for end users. In the development environment, CloudKit creates record types automatically on first use. Before submitting to the App Store: Run your app at least once in the development environment so that CloudKit creates the necessary record types. Open CloudKit Console. Select your container. Go to Schema → Deploy to Production. Review and deploy. The production schema cannot be modified once deployed (only extended with new fields), so test thoroughly in development first. Sharing with CKShare For apps that share data between users via CKShare:  Implement CloudKitFileSystemDelegate to handle share setup and participant management. Data Security Ensembles stores all sync data as CKAsset binary files attached to CloudKit records — not as structured record fields. The contents are opaque serialized event logs that are not human-readable, even without encryption. CloudKit encrypts all data at rest on Apple’s servers. With Advanced Data Protection (ADP) enabled, CKAsset data is end-to-end encrypted — Apple cannot read it. Without ADP, Apple manages the encryption keys and could theoretically access the data. For apps that need end-to-end encryption regardless of the user’s ADP setting, wrap the CloudKit backend with EnsemblesEncrypted:  This encrypts data with AES-256-GCM before it ever reaches CloudKit, so security does not depend on Apple’s infrastructure. Custom Zones By default, CloudKitFileSystem creates a custom record zone in the private database for efficient change tracking via CKServerChangeToken. The zone is created automatically on first connection. Delegate Implement CloudKitFileSystemDelegate to customize CloudKit behavior, handle errors, and manage sharing:
title: EnsemblesCloudKit
summary: A CloudKit-based cloud backend for Ensembles.
